Alright, welcome back. Here's the deal. Now that you know the three basic ten-inch to the front half of the straight line system, the next obvious question is why is every sale the same? You see, when I said to the Stratonites that night, they were like, what are you talking about? Every sale is not the same, Jordan. Every sale is different. And to be honest, you're probably thinking the same thing yourself right now. You're saying, you know, Jordan, you're totally wrong about this one. I mean, I see what you're saying about the other stuff, but every sale's got to be different. People have different needs, different values, different pain points, different beliefs. And my answer to you is, yeah, all of that is completely true, but it's simply not what I'm talking about. You see, when I said that every sale is the same, when I meant that night, and what turned out to be probably the most profound revelation I've ever had in my life, especially when it comes to selling, is that despite all those differences I just mentioned, people's needs, their objectives, their values, their pain points, all that individual stuff, the same three core elements must still line up in any prospect's mind before you have any shot of closing them. Let me repeat that. The reason that every sale is the same is because despite all that individual stuff, the same three core elements still have to line up in a prospect's mind before you have any shot of closing them. And it doesn't matter what you're selling, how you're selling it, how much the product costs, how much money your prospect has in his pocket, whether it's a tangible product or an intangible product like an idea, it can be over the phone in person, or if the person thinks their spouse will be pissed at them, or if they'll jump for joy when they walk through the door. None of it matters. If in a single moment in time you can create these three crucial elements in your prospect's mind, then bam, you have an excellent shot of closing them. And conversely, if even one of these three elements is missing, then you basically have no shot of closing them at all. Here's what they are. We call these three core elements the three tens, and the context here is a scale of certainty in your prospect's mind that runs from a one to a ten with a ten being the most certain a prospect could possibly be, and a one being the least certain they could possibly be. So again, if a prospect is currently at a ten, then it means that he or she is in a state of absolute certainty at that moment. And if they're currently at a one, then they're in a state of absolute uncertainty at that moment. Now watch, if a one represents absolute uncertainty, and a ten represents absolute certainty, then right here at the very beginning of the sale, when a prospect first enters the encounter and you haven't even said a word to them yet, they're typically going to have some amount of uncertainty about your product. And hence, they'll usually be somewhere in the middle about things, around a four, five, or a six, although theoretically they could be anywhere. But either way, whatever they happen to be at that moment, the point is that their initial level of certainty when they first enter the encounter is a direct result of their preconceived notions. And they're based on things like their initial gut reaction when they first lay eyes on your product. Or perhaps they've done some research about your product, looked at other similar products. Or maybe they have some preconceived notions about you, the salesperson. And of course, it could be any of those things individually, or it could be all of them working together. And then over here at the very end of the sale, the goal is to make them so absolutely certain about your product, they just say, yeah, I want to buy it. In other words, at the beginning of the sale, your prospects are typically going to be relatively uncertain about things, which is why they want to think about them. That's only natural, right? Then as you move them down the straight line from the opening to the close, everything you say, everything you do is designed to make them absolutely certain about what you're offering them that's the best fit, which makes them want to buy it. You see, people don't buy when they're uncertain, right? And that's the way it should be, right? So what you're doing here, what's happening here, as you're moving someone down the straight line from the opening to the close, you're moving them from a state of relative uncertainty to a state of absolute certainty. That's your goal. Now that being said, there's one very important point I want to raise here before we move on. Namely, there are actually two types of certainty. You have logical certainty and you have emotional certainty and they're entirely different things. See, logical certainty is that empirical logical perspective that does the idea make sense. Does it add up? Does the value proposition stack up when you essentially step back and look down at it from a sober, emotionless perspective? Does the features and benefits essentially make sense? Is the cost of benefit ratio tipped in your favor? That's logical certainty and your brain sort of weighing everything and based on everything you've seen in the past in your life, it's a judgment call that it does make sense. Now just one more thing about this type of certainty is that when you're creating an airtight logical case in your prospect, the key is that when someone is really at a very high level of logical certainty, they can actually tell the story to someone else from start to finish without missing any beats. They understand what's going on so they can tell the story to someone else. That's very important because what happens is someone's going to buy something and then go home and talk to their husband or wife and if they don't, they don't explain it like, oh boy, here we go and they might get pitch-lad. So when you have that advantage of your prospect really understanding from a sober, emotionless perspective of how your product works, why it makes so much sense, why it's such a great deal and it's like boom, boom, A, B, C, that's a really high level of logical certainty and it's crucial. Now on the flip side of the equation, you have what's called emotional certainty, which is an entirely different thing. It's based on a gut feeling. Something must be good. I got to have it. In other words, when you are emotionally certain, it doesn't matter what something costs, you just want to have it because you imagine yourself in the future using this product and feeling good. Whatever pain you had has been relieved. If you're feeling out of control, is what you're backing control causes this product. So when you're emotionally certain, you actually essentially future pace yourself as the phrase goes, you put yourself in the future with the sales and put you in the future by using language patterns and explains how great something will feel. Let's use cause for example and just imagine how great you'll feel three months down the road. You're driving down the hallway, the wind blowing in your hair, you're feeling sexy, powerful, you get that. That's emotional certainty. Now here's the question for you. Which of these two types of certainties do you think is more important when it comes to closing the sale? Is it logical certainty or emotional certainty? Take it through and tell me which one you think is more powerful, which gets the job done. Okay, fair enough. Logical certainty is a very good guess, but unfortunately you happen to be wrong. Okay, great. Emotional certainty is a great guess, but unfortunately you happen to be wrong. You see, it was another one of my trick questions. The truth is, if you want to close at the absolute highest level, you must have both logical certainty present at the very high level and also emotional certainty present at a very, very high level. See, here's the deal. People don't buy on logic. They buy on emotion and justify their decisions with logic. But if you don't create the logical case first, what happens is the logical mind serves as a human bullshit detector, essentially stopping someone from getting swept away emotionally if the sales person hasn't connected all the dots for them first. So in other words, in the absence of someone having a logical case being made, their mind says, bullshit, bullshit, bullshit, I didn't understand how this works. They're trying to tell me how great I'll feel in the future. So in other words, you can't sweep someone away emotionally without having the logical case being the underpinning first. Emotion, so the yeah, wow, you got to buy it, they must have it. That's where the power is. But only after you've created that airtight logical case first, which then frees a prospect up to be moved emotionally. Once you have both of those in place, wow, you can truly get to the point where you'll close anyone who's closeable. And that's what the straight line is all about. Make sense? Okay, great. So let's stop right now with a quick quiz. And when you come back, we'll keep plowing forward. I'll see you soon.
